steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main idea of the passage. The passage explains why The Yellow Admiral's abrupt ending is not a flaw: the entire Aubrey/Maturin series should be understood as one long work, not as independent books.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Over time, the Aubrey/Maturin series has acquired the same eminence among critics as works like *In Search of Lost Time* that have similar structures.: The passage compares structure only; it makes no claim about critical status or prestige. ✗
  • Choice B — A certain understanding of the structures of the novels in the Aubrey/Maturin series is helpful if one wishes to appreciate the series.: This captures the core point: the series must be understood as a single long work (not independent books) to make sense of its structure. ✓
  • Choice C — Many readers find the Aubrey/Maturin novels to be remarkably entertaining despite flaws in the novels' structures.: The passage argues the abrupt endings are not flaws when the series is read correctly. The idea of "flaws" contradicts the passage's point. ✗
  • Choice D — *The Yellow Admiral* and *The Hundred Days* are two of the most complex of O'Brian's novels due in part to their unconventional structures.: The passage never claims these two books are the most complex, and it attributes the abrupt endings to series structure, not unconventional design. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
